headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no family gets full military honors as hundreds refuse to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: New DOJ fraud division launched to tackle 'insane' federal program abuse costing billions
headlines.domestic: Americans expect AI to transform everything, but most find it useless in daily life right now

metadata.daily_do: Check if there's an unclaimed veteran burial in your area this month and show up

clips.caption: When the government gives money away like candy 🍭
Nobody should be buried alone after serving their country
Boss yelled 'potato' at Irish employee for workplace harassment

script.text: So let me get this straight — we've got fake homeless shelters run by guys from Philly getting four million dollars, and meanwhile I had to submit seventeen forms to get my VA disability rating increased by ten percent.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th. Let's get into it.

Three headlines this morning. First, geopolitics — and I'm stretching that category here, but this matters. Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman had no known family to claim his remains in Tennessee. So hundreds of community members and fellow veterans showed up to give him full military honors at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery.
Oh wow.
That's beautiful, man.
The VA rep said quote, when the paperwork came across my desk marked unclaimed veteran, I say that's incorrect. Thanks to all the support here today, we are able to claim our honorable veterans.
Jesus. I'm not crying at 9 AM.

Economy — Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division targeting what he calls insane levels of fraud in federal programs. The focus is Minnesota, where billions in taxpayer funds have been mishandled.
How many billions?

He didn't give a total number, but he cited cases like two Pennsylvania men who got four million in federal funds to run homeless shelters in Minneapolis that didn't exist. Also fake daycare centers billing for thousands of kids.
Four million? For fake shelters?
Blanche said the federal government gives money away like candy with no checks. Quote, the feds get a bill saying send us 2.9 million this month for school lunches, turns out it's mostly made up.
I can't even get Venmo to work half the time and these guys are running a four million dollar scam from their couch.
Domestic — a new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to significantly transform American life. But only forty-seven percent find AI useful in their daily lives right now.
That tracks.
Eighty percent are concerned AI is eroding trust in news and social media. And here's a fun one — seventy-four percent of Americans believe life exists or existed on other planets.
Well naturally. The universe is rather large.
I'm more surprised twenty-six percent think we're alone. Like, have you seen the size of space? That's some main character energy right there.
Alright, let's dig into this fraud story because, um, I think it reveals something bigger about how our government actually works. Duke, you served — does this surprise you?
Honestly? Not even a little bit. When I was doing contracting work after the Army, I saw this stuff constantly. The government has no idea how to verify anything.

Okay but hold on — that's not really fair. These programs exist because people are actually homeless, kids actually need lunch, daycares actually need funding. The fraud doesn't mean the need isn't real.
Nobody's saying the need isn't real, Bree. I'm saying when you make it this easy to steal, you're actually hurting the people who need help.
But the alternative is what — make it so hard to get help that legitimate organizations can't access funding either?
The alternative is basic oversight. Like, I don't know, maybe visit the homeless shelter before you send them four million dollars?
Right? Google Street View is free, guys. Just check if there's actually a building there.
Bree, what's your take on Blanche's approach here? He's saying no fraud too small, even prosecuting thousand-dollar cases.

I mean, I get the deterrent effect. But prosecuting every small fraud case is expensive. We're talking about court costs, legal fees, incarceration — it might cost more to prosecute than the actual fraud amount.
But that's exactly the thinking that got us here. Oh, it's only a thousand dollars, not worth pursuing. Oh, it's only ten thousand. Oh, it's only a hundred thousand.
That's— okay, that's a fair point.

Look, I had to provide my DD-214, medical records, witness statements, and a blood sample to get my VA benefits. Meanwhile these guys in Philly just said hey, we run homeless shelters, and the government said here's four million, have a nice day.
Wait, they made you give blood?
I'm exaggerating. Barely.
But here's what I'm curious about — why Minnesota specifically? Blanche keeps mentioning Minnesota. Is there something structural about how Minnesota runs these programs?
Minnesota has really generous social programs compared to other states. Which is good! But it might make them a target for fraud if the oversight systems aren't keeping up with the funding levels.
Or maybe Minnesota just got caught first. You think this is only happening in Minnesota? Come on.
That's actually terrifying to think about.
The timing is interesting too. This is one of the first major initiatives from the new DOJ. What signal does that send?
That they're tired of getting robbed by guys from Philly?
I think it sends the right signal. Like, we're actually going to pay attention to where taxpayer money goes. Crazy concept.

I just hope they don't use fraud as an excuse to gut legitimate programs. Because that's usually what happens — fraud gets discovered, entire program gets defunded, people who actually needed help get screwed.
Then maybe we should have been more careful about the fraud in the first place.
Alright, we'll see how this plays out. Professor, what's your take on federal law enforcement expanding into new areas?

Well Leo, this reminds me quite directly of why we created the Federal Bureau of Investigation in the first place, back in 1908. People forget — the FBI wasn't born to chase bank robbers or communists. It was created because federal programs were being systematically defrauded.

Theodore Roosevelt was president, and his administration was hemorrhaging money through fraudulent land deals, postal schemes, and customs corruption. The existing federal marshals couldn't handle the scope.
I didn't know that's why the FBI started.
Oh yes. Roosevelt's Attorney General Charles Bonaparte — Napoleon's grandnephew, incidentally — argued that you cannot run a modern government if you cannot trust your own programs. Sound familiar?

The fascinating parallel is that then, as now, the fraud was enabled by rapid expansion of federal programs without corresponding oversight mechanisms. The Homestead Act was creating opportunities for land fraud. The postal service was expanding faster than they could police it.
So this has happened before.

Multiple times. After World War II, we had massive fraud in veteran benefits and housing programs. During the Great Society expansion in the 1960s, Medicare and Medicaid fraud exploded within five years of creation.

The pattern is always the same — government creates new programs with noble intentions, bad actors immediately exploit the gaps, public gets outraged, enforcement gets strengthened. Then the cycle repeats with the next crisis.
So basically we never learn?
We learn. We just forget. Each generation of administrators believes they're smarter than the last, that modern technology makes fraud impossible. Meanwhile, fraudsters adapt faster than bureaucrats.

In 1908, land fraudsters used fake telegraph messages and forged surveyor reports. In 2026, they use fake websites and fabricated nonprofit registrations. The tools change. The incentives remain identical.
So what's the historical lesson here? Is this new DOJ division likely to work?

If they follow the 1908 model, yes. Bonaparte's team succeeded because they combined aggressive prosecution with systematic reform of the underlying programs. They didn't just chase fraudsters — they fixed the systems that made fraud easy.

But if they only focus on prosecution without reform, they'll be playing whack-a-mole indefinitely. You can prosecute every fraudster in America, but if the programs remain fundamentally exploitable, new fraudsters will simply take their place.
That's exactly what I was worried about.

The other lesson from 1908 is that public support matters enormously. Roosevelt succeeded because voters were genuinely outraged by the fraud. When that outrage fades, enforcement budgets get cut and we're back where we started.
Well, four million dollars for fake homeless shelters should keep people outraged for a while.

You'd think so. But Americans have remarkably short attention spans for government waste. By 1912, Roosevelt's fraud crackdown was old news. By 1920, we were back to massive federal corruption under Harding.

Alright, time for Rapid Fire, where we cover the news that makes you question everything. First up — Palantir, the company that builds AI weapons systems to track terrorists, has now deployed that same technology to help government workers find office chairs.
I'm sorry, what?

Yeah, apparently the government was spending too much on office furniture, so they're using military-grade AI to optimize chair purchases. From hunting Al-Qaeda to hunting ergonomic seating solutions.
That's actually kind of brilliant though? Like, if you're going to waste money on defense contractors, at least get some office chairs out of it.
I'm picturing a drone strike but it's just delivering a Herman Miller chair.
Next story — an Irish employee in the UK won a workplace harassment case after his boss repeatedly shouted the word potato at him. The tribunal ruled this was ethnic harassment.
Good heavens.
I mean, yeah, that's definitely harassment. But also, what kind of boss just yells potato at people?
A bad one! The employee said his boss would literally walk by his desk and just shout POTATO! Like some kind of vegetable-based psychological warfare.
This is why we need better management training. Like, there's a whole HR course called Don't Yell Vegetables at Your Employees.

Speaking of questionable authority figures — Louisiana classrooms are required to post the Ten Commandments by this Friday. Schools are scrambling to figure out logistics, like what font size and where to hang them.
I feel like there are more pressing issues in Louisiana education than poster placement.
Look, I'm not opposed to the Ten Commandments, but shouldn't we focus on making sure kids can read them first?
Plot twist — what if they post them in Comic Sans? Is that blasphemy?
Young man, Comic Sans is always blasphemy.
And finally, a Florida drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. The victim was only found when someone heard screaming from inside the unit.
Jesus Christ.
That's horrific. How do you even— months?
Yeah, this went from Florida Man to Florida Horror Movie real quick. The guy's okay now, but like, imagine being the person who rented the unit next door and just hearing muffled screaming for weeks.
That's enough Florida for today.
Florida: where office chairs get AI optimization and drug debts get medieval solutions.
Alright, time for the Daily Do. After hearing about Lonnie Wayman this morning, here's something you can actually act on.

Check if there's an unclaimed veteran burial in your area this month and show up. Most veterans' cemeteries post these notices online or you can call and ask. It takes an hour of your time and means everything to someone who served.
That's a good one, Leo. Nobody should be buried alone after serving their country.
I'm actually tearing up about this. Like, the fact that hundreds of people showed up for someone they didn't even know.

As the ancient Greeks understood, a society is judged not by how it treats its powerful, but by how it honors its forgotten. Today's quote comes from General Omar Bradley — The way to get started is to quit talking and begin doing.

I'm Jax Mooney reminding you that somewhere in America right now, a government contractor is using AI designed for counterterrorism to comparison shop office chairs, and honestly, that's the most American thing I've heard all week. We'll see you tomorrow.
